up.method: HPLC (Aminex-87H); uptake rate = slope of [glucose] vs cell density

sec.method: GC-FID (ZB-WaxPlus; ethanol co-eluted with MOPS on HPLC so measured by GC)

mfa.note: Fig. 1 FBA flux map for V. fischeri; per-product relative fluxes only in figure image; absolute values in SI Appendix Table S6

growth_records.cite: Mueller, Elliott P. et al. Metabolic controls on the carbon isotope fractionations of bacterial fermentation. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America (2025). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2511040122. [PMC12541407]
growth_records.snippet: Growth rates for E. coli, V. fischeri, C. pasteurianum are similar at 0.47, 0.434, and 0.473 h-1. ... In the case of V. fischeri, ethanol and MOPS coeluted, precluding HPLC quantification of ethanol in those samples. To circumvent this issu
growth_records.notes: mu=0.434 h-1 reported (OD600, SI Fig. S1). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.434 = 1.597 h. Strain MJ11; MOPS buffer pH 7.2. Anaerobic replicate was DUPLICATE (per Results/figure captions), others triplicate. Products measured by HPLC (+GC-FID for ethanol). Full medium composition in SI Tables S1-S5 -> not stated. Absolute rates in SI Table S6; rate fields null (not invented). Growth temperature not stated in main text.

growth_records.cite: Mueller, Elliott P. et al. Metabolic controls on the carbon isotope fractionations of bacterial fermentation. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America (2025). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2511040122. [PMC12541407]
growth_records.snippet: Comparison of experimental results obtained from three fermenting organisms and five total conditions, including aerobic and anaerobic growth of E. coli and V. fischeri.
growth_records.notes: Distinct aerobic condition for V. fischeri MJ11 explicitly reported in Fig. 3A. Products measured by HPLC/GC-FID. Separate aerobic growth rate not reported in accessible main text -> growth_rate_per_h null (only generic mu=0.434 stated). Absolute rates in SI; rate fields null (not invented).
